mnemonics: Pressing multiple buttons on a TV remote might give you a garbled channel (invalid output), just like a simple encoder does with multiple active inputs.
keyPoints: An encoder converts 2^n inputs into an n-bit binary code, where only one input should be active at a time.
A priority encoder solves the 'multiple active inputs' problem by outputting the code for the highest-priority input.
The output of an encoder is the binary equivalent of the index of the active input line.
commonMistakes: Assuming an encoder can handle multiple active inputs correctly (it can't, without priority logic).
Forgetting that the 'valid' output (V) is crucial for a priority encoder to indicate when at least one input is active.
